questions.question_text: Which statement best describes light and straight-line travel in light: mirrors and lenses?

options.text: Reflection is the return of light into the original medium after it strikes a surface.
Light travels in straight lines through a uniform medium until its path is changed.
The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence when both are measured from the normal.
A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image of the same size as the object at an equal distance behind the mirror.

questions.explanation: Light travels in straight lines through a uniform medium until its path is changed.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with light and straight-line travel?

options.text: A ray striking at 30 degrees to the normal reflects at 30 degrees to the normal.
A mirror reflects light toward an observer.
A pinhole can form an image because light rays travel through the small opening.
A person sees an upright image in a flat dressing mirror.

questions.explanation: A pinhole can form an image because light rays travel through the small opening.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes light and straight-line travel from a nearby idea?

options.text: The image is virtual because reflected rays only appear to come from behind the mirror.
Reflection changes direction without requiring the light to pass through the reflecting surface.
Angles are measured from the normal line, not from the mirror surface.
A light ray is a model of direction, not a visible thread connecting source and object.

questions.explanation: A light ray is a model of direction, not a visible thread connecting source and object.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of light and straight-line travel?

options.text: Use a ray diagram to predict whether an object can be seen from a position.
Draw the incident and reflected rays to explain how an object is seen in a mirror.
Add a normal at the point of incidence before measuring either angle.
Use the equal-distance rule to locate a plane-mirror image.

questions.explanation: Use a ray diagram to predict whether an object can be seen from a position.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about light and straight-line travel?

options.text: A rough surface can reflect light even when it does not form a clear image.
Light does not bend simply because a diagram line is drawn at an angle.
Using the mirror surface as the reference gives the wrong angle relationship.
A plane-mirror image is not formed on a screen placed behind the mirror.

questions.explanation: Light does not bend simply because a diagram line is drawn at an angle.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining light and straight-line travel. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence when both are measured from the normal.
Reflection is the return of light into the original medium after it strikes a surface.
Light travels in straight lines through a uniform medium until its path is changed.
A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image of the same size as the object at an equal distance behind the mirror.

questions.explanation: Light travels in straight lines through a uniform medium until its path is changed.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about light and straight-line travel?

options.text: A person sees an upright image in a flat dressing mirror.
A mirror reflects light toward an observer.
A ray striking at 30 degrees to the normal reflects at 30 degrees to the normal.
A pinhole can form an image because light rays travel through the small opening.

questions.explanation: A pinhole can form an image because light rays travel through the small opening.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

options.text: Use a ray diagram to predict whether an object can be seen from a position.
Draw the incident and reflected rays to explain how an object is seen in a mirror.

options.text: Reflection changes direction without requiring the light to pass through the reflecting surface.
A light ray is a model of direction, not a visible thread connecting source and object.

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate light and straight-line travel?

options.text: Add a normal at the point of incidence before measuring either angle.
Draw the incident and reflected rays to explain how an object is seen in a mirror.
Use a ray diagram to predict whether an object can be seen from a position.
Use the equal-distance rule to locate a plane-mirror image.

questions.explanation: Use a ray diagram to predict whether an object can be seen from a position.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes reflection in light: mirrors and lenses?

options.text: Lateral inversion is the apparent left-right reversal seen in a plane mirror.
The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence when both are measured from the normal.
A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image of the same size as the object at an equal distance behind the mirror.
Reflection is the return of light into the original medium after it strikes a surface.

questions.explanation: Reflection is the return of light into the original medium after it strikes a surface.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with reflection?

options.text: A mirror reflects light toward an observer.
A ray striking at 30 degrees to the normal reflects at 30 degrees to the normal.
A person sees an upright image in a flat dressing mirror.
The word ambulance is printed reversed on the front of a vehicle so it reads correctly in mirrors.

questions.explanation: A mirror reflects light toward an observer.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes reflection from a nearby idea?

options.text: Angles are measured from the normal line, not from the mirror surface.
Reflection changes direction without requiring the light to pass through the reflecting surface.
The image is virtual because reflected rays only appear to come from behind the mirror.
The mirror does not swap up and down; it reverses the apparent side-to-side orientation.

questions.explanation: Reflection changes direction without requiring the light to pass through the reflecting surface.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of reflection?

options.text: Use the equal-distance rule to locate a plane-mirror image.
Add a normal at the point of incidence before measuring either angle.
Draw the incident and reflected rays to explain how an object is seen in a mirror.
Compare a labelled object and its mirror image to identify the reversed side.

questions.explanation: Draw the incident and reflected rays to explain how an object is seen in a mirror.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about reflection?

options.text: A mirror image is not a simple rotation of the object.
Using the mirror surface as the reference gives the wrong angle relationship.
A plane-mirror image is not formed on a screen placed behind the mirror.
A rough surface can reflect light even when it does not form a clear image.

questions.explanation: A rough surface can reflect light even when it does not form a clear image.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

options.text: Reflection is the return of light into the original medium after it strikes a surface.
The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence when both are measured from the normal.

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate reflection?

options.text: Draw the incident and reflected rays to explain how an object is seen in a mirror.

options.text: A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image of the same size as the object at an equal distance behind the mirror.
The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence when both are measured from the normal.
Lateral inversion is the apparent left-right reversal seen in a plane mirror.

options.text: The word ambulance is printed reversed on the front of a vehicle so it reads correctly in mirrors.
A person sees an upright image in a flat dressing mirror.
A ray striking at 30 degrees to the normal reflects at 30 degrees to the normal.
A concave mirror can converge parallel rays near its principal focus.

questions.explanation: A ray striking at 30 degrees to the normal reflects at 30 degrees to the normal.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes angles of reflection from a nearby idea?

options.text: Concave and convex shapes produce different ray behaviour and image possibilities.
The image is virtual because reflected rays only appear to come from behind the mirror.
The mirror does not swap up and down; it reverses the apparent side-to-side orientation.
Angles are measured from the normal line, not from the mirror surface.

options.text: Add a normal at the point of incidence before measuring either angle.
Use the equal-distance rule to locate a plane-mirror image.
Compare a labelled object and its mirror image to identify the reversed side.
Use a principal axis and representative rays when drawing a spherical-mirror diagram.

options.text: A plane-mirror image is not formed on a screen placed behind the mirror.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes plane mirrors in light: mirrors and lenses?

options.text: Refraction is the change in direction of light when it passes between transparent media because its speed changes.
Lateral inversion is the apparent left-right reversal seen in a plane mirror.
Spherical mirrors are curved mirror surfaces that can be concave or convex.
A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image of the same size as the object at an equal distance behind the mirror.

questions.explanation: A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image of the same size as the object at an equal distance behind the mirror.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with plane mirrors?

options.text: A person sees an upright image in a flat dressing mirror.
The word ambulance is printed reversed on the front of a vehicle so it reads correctly in mirrors.
A concave mirror can converge parallel rays near its principal focus.
A pencil appears bent where it enters water.

questions.explanation: A person sees an upright image in a flat dressing mirror.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes plane mirrors from a nearby idea?

options.text: The mirror does not swap up and down; it reverses the apparent side-to-side orientation.
The image is virtual because reflected rays only appear to come from behind the mirror.
Concave and convex shapes produce different ray behaviour and image possibilities.
The apparent bend occurs at a boundary between media, not because the pencil itself changes shape.

questions.explanation: The image is virtual because reflected rays only appear to come from behind the mirror.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of plane mirrors?

options.text: Use a principal axis and representative rays when drawing a spherical-mirror diagram.
Compare a labelled object and its mirror image to identify the reversed side.
Use the equal-distance rule to locate a plane-mirror image.
Draw the ray before and after the boundary when explaining an apparent shift.

questions.explanation: Use the equal-distance rule to locate a plane-mirror image.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about plane mirrors?

options.text: Refraction does not mean that light is reflected back from every boundary.
A mirror image is not a simple rotation of the object.
A curved mirror cannot always be treated like a plane mirror.
A plane-mirror image is not formed on a screen placed behind the mirror.

questions.explanation: A plane-mirror image is not formed on a screen placed behind the mirror.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

options.text: A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image of the same size as the object at an equal distance behind the mirror.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes lateral inversion in light: mirrors and lenses?

options.text: Spherical mirrors are curved mirror surfaces that can be concave or convex.
Lateral inversion is the apparent left-right reversal seen in a plane mirror.
Refraction is the change in direction of light when it passes between transparent media because its speed changes.
A lens is a transparent curved object that refracts light to converge or diverge rays.

questions.explanation: Lateral inversion is the apparent left-right reversal seen in a plane mirror.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with lateral inversion?

options.text: A pencil appears bent where it enters water.
A concave mirror can converge parallel rays near its principal focus.
The word ambulance is printed reversed on the front of a vehicle so it reads correctly in mirrors.
A convex lens can focus sunlight to a bright spot.

questions.explanation: The word ambulance is printed reversed on the front of a vehicle so it reads correctly in mirrors.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes lateral inversion from a nearby idea?

options.text: A convex lens generally converges parallel rays, while a concave lens generally diverges them.
Concave and convex shapes produce different ray behaviour and image possibilities.
The apparent bend occurs at a boundary between media, not because the pencil itself changes shape.
The mirror does not swap up and down; it reverses the apparent side-to-side orientation.

questions.explanation: The mirror does not swap up and down; it reverses the apparent side-to-side orientation.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of lateral inversion?

options.text: Compare a labelled object and its mirror image to identify the reversed side.
Use a principal axis and representative rays when drawing a spherical-mirror diagram.
Draw the ray before and after the boundary when explaining an apparent shift.
Use a lens to form or enlarge an image only with safe light levels and proper supervision.

questions.explanation: Compare a labelled object and its mirror image to identify the reversed side.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about lateral inversion?

options.text: A curved mirror cannot always be treated like a plane mirror.
A mirror image is not a simple rotation of the object.
Refraction does not mean that light is reflected back from every boundary.
A lens does not magnify every object in every position.

questions.explanation: A mirror image is not a simple rotation of the object.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes spherical mirrors in light: mirrors and lenses?

options.text: An image forms where light rays from an object meet or appear to meet after reflection or refraction.
Refraction is the change in direction of light when it passes between transparent media because its speed changes.
A lens is a transparent curved object that refracts light to converge or diverge rays.
Spherical mirrors are curved mirror surfaces that can be concave or convex.

questions.explanation: Spherical mirrors are curved mirror surfaces that can be concave or convex.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with spherical mirrors?

options.text: A concave mirror can converge parallel rays near its principal focus.
A pencil appears bent where it enters water.
A convex lens can focus sunlight to a bright spot.
A convex lens can form an image on a screen when the object is placed suitably.

questions.explanation: A concave mirror can converge parallel rays near its principal focus.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes spherical mirrors from a nearby idea?

options.text: The apparent bend occurs at a boundary between media, not because the pencil itself changes shape.
Concave and convex shapes produce different ray behaviour and image possibilities.
A convex lens generally converges parallel rays, while a concave lens generally diverges them.
Real images can be caught on a screen; virtual images cannot be caught directly on a screen.

questions.explanation: Concave and convex shapes produce different ray behaviour and image possibilities.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of spherical mirrors?

options.text: Use a lens to form or enlarge an image only with safe light levels and proper supervision.
Draw the ray before and after the boundary when explaining an apparent shift.
Use a principal axis and representative rays when drawing a spherical-mirror diagram.
Move the screen until the rays converge to test for a real image.

questions.explanation: Use a principal axis and representative rays when drawing a spherical-mirror diagram.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about spherical mirrors?

options.text: A sharp image seen in a mirror is not automatically a real image.
Refraction does not mean that light is reflected back from every boundary.
A lens does not magnify every object in every position.
A curved mirror cannot always be treated like a plane mirror.

questions.explanation: A curved mirror cannot always be treated like a plane mirror.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

A lens is a transparent curved object that refracts light to converge or diverge rays.
An image forms where light rays from an object meet or appear to meet after reflection or refraction.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes refraction in light: mirrors and lenses?

options.text: A lens is a transparent curved object that refracts light to converge or diverge rays.
Refraction is the change in direction of light when it passes between transparent media because its speed changes.
An image forms where light rays from an object meet or appear to meet after reflection or refraction.
Safe light investigations control brightness, distance, and exposure to protect eyes and skin.

questions.explanation: Refraction is the change in direction of light when it passes between transparent media because its speed changes.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with refraction?

options.text: A convex lens can form an image on a screen when the object is placed suitably.
A convex lens can focus sunlight to a bright spot.
A pencil appears bent where it enters water.
A classroom light box is safer to inspect than looking directly at the Sun.

questions.explanation: A pencil appears bent where it enters water.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes refraction from a nearby idea?

options.text: Studying light does not require staring at an intense source.
A convex lens generally converges parallel rays, while a concave lens generally diverges them.
Real images can be caught on a screen; virtual images cannot be caught directly on a screen.
The apparent bend occurs at a boundary between media, not because the pencil itself changes shape.

questions.explanation: The apparent bend occurs at a boundary between media, not because the pencil itself changes shape.

Never look directly at the Sun through a lens, mirror, or optical instrument.

options.text: Safe light investigations control brightness, distance, and exposure to protect eyes and skin.
An image forms where light rays from an object meet or appear to meet after reflection or refraction.
Light travels in straight lines through a uniform medium until its path is changed.
Reflection is the return of light into the original medium after it strikes a surface.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with image formation?

options.text: A pinhole can form an image because light rays travel through the small opening.
A classroom light box is safer to inspect than looking directly at the Sun.
A convex lens can form an image on a screen when the object is placed suitably.
A mirror reflects light toward an observer.

questions.source_locator: Chapter 10; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes image formation from a nearby idea?

options.text: Reflection changes direction without requiring the light to pass through the reflecting surface.
Studying light does not require staring at an intense source.
A light ray is a model of direction, not a visible thread connecting source and object.
Real images can be caught on a screen; virtual images cannot be caught directly on a screen.

A rough surface can reflect light even when it does not form a clear image.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes optical safety in light: mirrors and lenses?

options.text: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence when both are measured from the normal.
Light travels in straight lines through a uniform medium until its path is changed.
Reflection is the return of light into the original medium after it strikes a surface.
Safe light investigations control brightness, distance, and exposure to protect eyes and skin.

questions.explanation: Safe light investigations control brightness, distance, and exposure to protect eyes and skin.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with optical safety?

options.text: A classroom light box is safer to inspect than looking directly at the Sun.
A pinhole can form an image because light rays travel through the small opening.
A mirror reflects light toward an observer.
A ray striking at 30 degrees to the normal reflects at 30 degrees to the normal.

questions.explanation: A classroom light box is safer to inspect than looking directly at the Sun.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes optical safety from a nearby idea?

Reflection changes direction without requiring the light to pass through the reflecting surface.
